speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Helmer Ringsage took place at his home in Moscow on April 1, 1976. Interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: One thing about from when you were a kid that I wanted to ask you about. You said to me after your mother passed away that you kind of got some of the neighbors, some neighbor ladies were kind of looked after you kids some.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't you say that to me?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They kind of... Well... And I was wondering about that. Well, listen, that day that she died, there was two women come there and cleaned up the house.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That's all the help she got, or we got. The rest of it we had a bachelor get along the best we could. That's the way it was.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No, that's all the help we got there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what was it like afterwards with you guys having to do all that work? What was it like doing all that work yourself? Was that rough for the kids?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, we got along. We just kept working, kept struggling. All we could do.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Nothing else. Oh, daddy was, that's why I said, he was the best daddy ever was on this God's green earth. Because of that, he pulled us kids, he kept us children together.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Now, he took the two, I told you about that, he took the two younger ones over to Granbire's, didn't I?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No. Well, he took them over there, see. Julius and Joseph, they were the two youngest. He took them over to his mother and dad. And they stayed there, well, the young fella, Joseph, the youngest, he stayed there, I don't remember now, but quite a while. Several years, I think. And Julius, the other one, he come home, I think it was that Paul.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, let's see, yeah. That was 1901. Yeah, he come home, no, he didn't come, I can't remember too sure about that. He was there, he come home sooner anyway than the younger one did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your dad do the cooking then, your father?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, now there's a thing, let's see, wait a minute. 1901.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I said I was 13, no, Simon was old enough then.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Dad did a lot of it, but the brother Simon that died with cancer here a few years back, he was only six or seven years old.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He was put in the kitchen, he was a dependable boy. And when we boys would go out and shock grain in the harvest field, why, he was in to take care of this.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Daddy was smart enough to know a little bit about cooking, so he would say put on a soup bone to cook and some vegetables and Simon would tend to it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And then Daddy too had to bake bread and he learned me how to bake bread. When he couldn't do it, I had to do it. That's why I said I've been baking bread ever since I was 13.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That is off and on.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: When my wife was able, of course she done it, but when she got disabled, well she told me her way and I baked bread her way. And so that's the way we kept going.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You said too that your father made men out of you little devils. Yeah, he did. He was strict.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He was strict.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I told you about it where he gave me a lickin' when I had a fight with a kid, didn't I?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Uh -uh, no. Yeah. As a kid and I got into a scrape, see, coming home from school. And so he was smaller than I was, but he had two bigger brothers. And so I knew better than to hit him or do anything to him until he got too bad and he got to throwing rocks at me and hurt me with a rock.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I just grabbed him and laid him down, see, and that made him awful mad. And he knew what my dad was. And he went right by the home when he went, because they lived beyond us, went right by the home and he told my dad and he just grabbed me. And them days he pulled the pants down and give me a good spanking right before that kid.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, the next day I told it to his older brother, the one that General Tim, his name was, General is straighten things out. Now Ralph and I, we were pals.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But this Tim, he was a friend to this Albert Setlow, Mabel Setlow. He was a, that is, he was his girlfriend, you might say. So they were, he depended on what they'd say.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So I told him, I said, you asked Albert and Mabel who started this, Frank.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So when he asked them and they told him it was Joe, his brother. He went and slept the heck out of him, see. That's the way that wound up. And, but that's the way dad was. I said, that's all right. I didn't see into it then, but I took my medicine. Dad was the boss. And my brother did his dinner, he didn't, he didn't savv it that way, but I told him, I said, listen now, if we'd had lenient dad like hard ones that give him everything, I said, there they landed in jail. And they got thrown out of school and everything. I said, those kids didn't.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And not only that, I guess I've mentioned that before about the Baudeshariah and the Christian college there at Spokane.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Didn't I tell you that?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah. Well, you didn't really tell me about it though.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the deal on it?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, I told you that, yeah, I told you that. I thought that's why I wanted to say no, what you had marked down. We were repeating some of it. No, we didn't put that down at all. Well, in a way. You told to me, but it wasn't. Well, I'll tell you, here's what it was. They, in that school, these fellows that come around to organize this, that was before they built the scholarly seat. They wanted any parent that would donate money to help build it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And dad was a, dad and all his relatives are pretty much offspring, you know, in the old country. I was just reading in the history of today, or the other day, I got it here, from the Scandinavian countries. They're 97 % Luther. And that's what he was.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And so, in a way, they said that any parent that would donate money could send their children there free of tuition.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So we played lucky.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: We got enough made money to send, that is to pay the board, the board was only at $2 .90 a week at that time.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: We got two rooms for $4 a month each. That's $8. Right at the college, only about two blocks away, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the name of the school?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It was Spokane College. That's the name. It was the name. But it broke down. But that's what it was. Spokane College, up there, it's about, I think it's six or eight blocks south of Manitou Park was. I don't know what they got there now. I haven't been back there since. I got pictures here I can show you of us in the Manitou Park, me and the students.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I better get them and show you. Okay. So you get a better idea. There's the girls. And here's us boys. Here's Steiner. Oh yeah, I'm over here. I weighed the most there of any year that I ever weighed. You see there, we just come off of the farm. We've been a batch. We got cooking there. See, they had a woman cooking there and knew how. And then we got a different diet entirely. And regulations more.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Going to school, see. Getting relaxed. Not the chill work that we had on the farm. I guess that's what done it. I just kept getting fatter and fatter.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I weighed 164, the very most I ever weighed. Because I wasn't doing heavy work, see. Just going to school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How old were you?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: 20 years old when we finished up the seventh and eighth grade. That's what I told you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you finish the seventh and eighth grade off of it?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: We did in eight months.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You see, we hadn't got it down on the ridge in the grade school. We was hit and miss. Maybe a couple months, three months as long as the schools would go.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But I was the one that was determined.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I told my brother, I said, boy, we got a little money, let's go up to Spokane College and finish up our courses.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Dad tried his best. Now that one thing he'd done, that was the making of us, you might say, to really line us up.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: If we hadn't had something back, I mean, determination, of course, we could have just wondered and done like that. But he didn't spoil us. He put his foot down every time we got a little fresh Foxy, you know. We just stepped to his music and my God, I'm glad he did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he do about this college? Was he for you guys?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No, he didn't do nothing only. He just donated money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, but I mean, as far as you guys, was he in favor of you going up there?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, he was in favor. He tried his best. Now this one brother that I...

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: when he died with cancer, he helped him out by, after us boys growed up and got to working out, he gave him a chance to go to Peck to finish his schooling.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And this little brother, doodles know it ain't no listen, the one next to the youngest, well, he helped both of them. That's right, the youngest one, when he, after he got moved to Kirkland, Washington, I guess that was when I...

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: let's see, yeah, that's when I was married and farming his big place. And he gave him, furnished him money to take up a trade of mechanics.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And the younger brother, he is, I mean the next younger one, Judas, he gave him a... a chance, yeah, it was on, when he did Seattle, that's what it was, my brother at Seattle had got started into a brick service station.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And Dad gave him a lift there. And today that kid has got more, he's got less education than either one of us. Only about the fifth or sixth grade, but he had to get up and go, and discipline from Dad, and today that man is, oh, he's worth thousands of dollars. He could have been luckier maybe to have married another woman, but by golly, they done pretty good. She was a table waiter, and he was running this brick service station. Then, when the war came, he took up this electric furnace equipment. He's smart enough to see into things. He got a course in that, just like us kids, to get this other little course. And he went through that, and, yeah, you got this on, yeah.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He went through that, oh, I'm going to put mine away, because I can warm it a little later. Okay, you can finish it now, man, we're still warm. We didn't put any of that on tape.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of Spokane? Was that the first time you'd ever been to the city?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, that was the first time we'd been up to Spokane, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of the city when you got up there?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It was kind of different from down around here. Well, I just didn't think much about it. We was busy going to school.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That don't amount to much.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I'd rather tell you about when we, I mean, how I got lined out making money to buy my home than that. I wasn't interested in Spokane at all at the time. Okay, we'll hit that this morning, but I do want to ask you what going to school there was like, what that college was like. Oh, I tell you, it was a nice Christian college, I'll tell you something. I got to tell you that. Every morning, Dr.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Gloskow was the president's name.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he had a choir, religious choir, every morning.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Now that's one blessed thing. We was brought up, you might say every morning we had this same as going to church. And he had this choir singing and he was the main leader of it. And I tell you, and then they had, I guess it was an organ playing.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Every morning, I don't remember, I think it was a half an hour or maybe more.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Every morning.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And say that, I enjoyed that. And then they learned, they even sent us down to town. There's a certain place in town where you could find out about sexual diseases.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: One place for the men and one place for the ladies. And I tell you, we was greenhorns, you see. Daddy never told us nothing. But we got in there and seen the, it just showed on plastic the different stages of different diseases. Now that was an awful good lesson. It just made men out of us in that respect, too. That's why I guess I was able to pick out a good companion when I did.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, you know how it is. When you're young, you can flirt with a lot of young girls.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I had lots of them. I could have got them.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No, they didn't shoot me until I found this particular one. I was going with them. From up here on Marysville.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know Daugherty's?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah. Well, it was Mrs. Daugherty's sister -in -law.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Clarence's sister.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I was going, you see there, we got acquainted in this short course.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I told you about the short course we were taking after we got through Spokane College, didn't I?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No, you didn't. Yes, I did. No, you didn't mention it, but we never put nothing about that. Oh, I thought you did. Well, anyway. Now, in Spokane, you stayed there at this course for about seven, eight months. Eight months, exactly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And did you have a teacher for each different course?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No, no, no. We just had the one teacher and she was thorough. She was a little Norwegian girl.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And now, Grammer, she just put the stuff on the blackboard and explained it thoroughly until I got that into my noodle. Well, on the ridge, I didn't know sickum about it because we didn't get there when the beginning of it and nothing.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So that was that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So she took you straight through and you learned?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, yeah. We got through the seventh and eighth grade.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many kids were there in that class?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, there wasn't too many. That was why they could, she was a private teacher, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: For us preparatory courses, what it's called.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to spend a lot of your time studying when you were out there?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, we had a room. We were lucky. We was rooming with one of the professors, Dr. Van de Valkur.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He was one of the professors in the college. And it was nice and quiet.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: We were on the upstairs floor, two rooms, my brother and I. And they had a little four -year -old boy, but of course, he squacked a little bit. He didn't bother us much. He was way downstairs. And two of them, not only that, but I took up a violin lesson. I bought a violin for $55. And there was a young man, only 14, he was just a kid.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He was a professional. He played in some band in Spokane. And he'd come and give me lessons for a dollar a lesson.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So I took that up, too, while I was there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you learn how to play by note?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But I didn't keep on practicing long enough to make it real good, you know. But I got so I could play several tunes by note.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And then, top it off, I finally, it was my risk to give out. See, I was too old when I started. And that's why I'm trying to tell all my great grandchildren and all of them, and my grandchildren, I told them, they've got to start early. The younger the better. They teach them now, they're three or four or five years old.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: When they get, if they got music in their head, but some of them if they hadn't, they'd just as well forget it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, there's one thing, when you say that they showed you the course of sexual diseases, was that like what they call sex education these days?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That's what it was.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Sure it was.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That's what it was. Because I'm surprised they had that back then. Oh my gosh, they had it not in the college, downtown, in Spokane.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But they knew where to send us kids. That's what I mean. That was another good thing about that college.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They really, and we met a lot of nice students there. Some of them from Montana, some of them from out here in Washington. And there was two, a brother and a sister from Moscow here.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No, there's three. There was Ryerson, Richard, and Ruth, brother and sister.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And then there was a preacher's daughter that used to be a preacher. Andresen, he used to be a Lutheran preacher, come clear to the park to them little churches.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It was his, I think it was his daughter, or his granddaughter, I don't remember which, went to school there.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And some of them cleared from out in Washington and out in the Big Bend country. We really got in with a good bunch.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They were all Christians of course. They'd been brought up in a church environment you might say.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So we really, I told my brother, my, I said we were lucky to get into such a good environment as that.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And when I told Dad about it, you know, he thought that was dandy that we had got into it. And went through like we did.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well this, I'm just, I wonder how they even taught the sex education in those days compared to the way they do it now. You know now it's in the schools more or less. They have classes in it so kids will have a little better idea of what it's about. They won't be entirely unprepared. Yeah. But did they actually have a teacher who gave you a lecture or explained how... Oh they lectured on several things. I can't just exactly quote all of that because I can't remember. But they was pretty good that way. Things that they thought was important, you know. Okay now you said about this short course.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now was that the one you did? Oh now you want to get on that?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah well, okay. Well I'll tell you.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Then that was the next year after we'd been to Spokane. We was in Spokane 1910 and 11. 1911 and 12 we come and took this short course in Moscow.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It was a six month course.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And the first year, did I show you the pictures of them students I went to school with at the university?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I'll show you them. So you can get an idea. Six month courses now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this a, what was the short course in? What was the subject?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Agriculture. And it was a kind of a dual education. It's what it was.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: We got some arithmetic, some soils erosion, I mean soils they called it. You know, studying of soils. And animal husbandry, stop judging.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And poultry.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And what else was it now?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh yeah, horticulture.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And plant life.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I think irrigation. See I've got that picture here.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I've got it in another place but it don't matter you can know that.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: There's a picture where I'm in a picture by the administration building. Maybe I better get you that and show you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where are you going there?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh we was renting. We was renting from, that year we was renting from a widow. She boarded and roomed us for $20 a month.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She was a widow with two boys down on the university, what do you call that place now?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It's down, Student Union Place, wherever that is.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And it's over there, I don't remember the main street but they call it Student Union Avenue I think. It's beyond 6th and so, And we all, there was about seven or eight of us rooming in that same house. He had a big house. He was from southern Idaho and he was a, she had a pyre and she was teaching her boys pyre lessons and he could play the pyre. So we had singing and we was all enjoying ourselves having a regular home sweet home place to live in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you get the, did you have any trouble with getting a 20 a month? Was that your money or your?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh we'd made that, my brother and I, after the first year that we took that agriculture course we was lucky we rented some bean ground over there on the ridge.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And we got $500 worth of beans.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: After you took the first short course?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well were these short courses good? Did they teach you a lot about that you then used in the farming?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well sure, it was, I told you, stock judging, soils, chemistry, poultry, and all that stuff. Well sometimes you know, sometimes they're good, sometimes they don't look good. Well this was good and I'll tell you who the professor was and he was a dandy, oh Professor Eddings, was our professor. Carlisle was the president of the university at the time.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Now that is 1911 and 12.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They're all gone of course.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, but that's the way it was.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And by golly I'll tell you right now that was just as good or better.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well if we hadn't went to Spokane and got that preparatory school we wouldn't have got as much out of it as we did.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh yeah and bookkeeping, I forgot to tell you that. I learned bookkeeping there, single entry. Now that was very interesting, and spelling, well they had just everything you might say in the preparatory line but most of it was in soils. This is university I'm talking now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well I noticed there were a couple girls there too, were they taking the farming courses?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No just one, she was taking domestic science. I see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, just the one girl, her brother was there too. They were from southern Idaho. Yeah we enjoyed that course. And let's see there's 11 and 12. Well the next year, that's when I started, the next winter, 12, we didn't go back no more see. We was there at 11 and 12, 1912 in the fall and 13 I went to the woods.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh yeah, and you told me about that. Yeah I went to the woods and couldn't, no at first I got a job with a prune job with, no, the first job I got was washing dishes down here at a potlatch hotel.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Forty dollars a month in boat. Well I couldn't do the dishes fast enough to suit them or I wasn't a dishwasher. And so they hired a little dago, they call it, Italian from Spokane and I was glad to quit. I didn't want it, you took me all day and all night to do the damn dishes you know and all that.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So I went out and handed me a job in the woods. I got up to what they called prune job. And I of course was raised on a farm I thought I wanted to drive horses. I told them I'd like to get on the sleigh -haul.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well he put me in the choppin' swampin' they call it. And I worked like a beaver because I wanted to hold my job and I was just, well I see 1912 I was 24.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You see, a young husky and he seen it and he put me on and workin' away, workin' away as hard as I could. But as he come fallin' he took a pity on me.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well I got to howlin' about, I tell ya them gypo places was bad, lousy.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I got a lice on me and you had to boil them down in a coal oil can, that's all they had in the camps, in them camps.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And they didn't have very good board there either in that gypo place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Very good what?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Boil.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So when I...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say boil them down what do you mean boil them down?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: The eggs that's in your clothes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You had to dip your clothes in there?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No you cook them, boil them. You had to boil them to get them cooked.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I mean to kill those and that. But you could get, if that new lumber that come in from campin' you had to sleep together, bunkin' together, double bunk they called it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You had to... Then finally they got a hold of an ointment, I put that on me, then I got so I could work and sleep. But I kept hollerin' anyway, I wanted to get out of there I guess and boss he took a pity on me, he seen I was willing, able to work and just wanted to try my best to get upper I guess. And as you say he said to the young feller, he says I believe you better go work in the potlatch company. They got a better camps.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He was honest about it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And they did have. I went there over to camp eight. I got the picture of that in the book here too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was the winter you worked over there with your brother?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No, no, no, no that was 36. No, well you told me there were two different winters you told me about. Well no it was just one winter. This was in the fall when we worked with my brother. 36th of fall. Yeah. But this was winter when I... Well it was fall when I come up to Bovill see. Startin' winter. And I worked... Yeah I sent for a brother, the one that had the... that died with cancer. Simon. He was 17 years old. Yeah, he told me. Yeah, he's the one I got that winter. Right, and you told me that. Yeah, that's the brother. Yeah I forgot. Anyway we saw it together for a while see. And we were... them days they logged with highline donkeys.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Way back in the hills, mountains.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And they would have a choker, several chokers, they'd haul 20, 15, 20, depending on the size of the logs, to a load on that highline. And they had a fellow, old George David, who was a kid, about 14 then. He was a whistle punk. He could... They had a wire run clear back up in the woods so he'd pull that cord one, give signals to this particular... This fellow that was hookin' these chokers. They called him Highline Man. And after he got everything hooked up, you know, he'd holler, well if you wanted him to stop he'd say, Hi! And if wanted to start, Hi! Hi! And, no, let's see, no, that's when he's loaded.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Hi! Hi! He just hoisted up gradual. And when he wanted to take it to the... down to the cars, to the... where they unloaded him, it was three highs. Hi! Hi! Hi! And the way he jerked them, that little three -ton, the engineer knew just what to do, see. That's the way they sickled him. And down they went.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well then,

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: we sawed a while like that.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And they'd give us an undercutter. He couldn't keep ahead of us. We was foolish for workin' so fast, but we did. Because them saws, they were good filers and they were nice, soft, white pine, you know, and we just...

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: My brother and I could saw like mad. He was husky, my 17 -year -old brother. He was become to be the biggest of all of us.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he was awful willing, nice to be with. So in a way, I told Simon and my brother I didn't like to have him run around. I went to Alperva to work. And, well, because of that, they switched us on different... I mean a different hoister. He was no good. He was no good. He was just a 16 -year -old kid. And they didn't want to farm because his dad was a good cook.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And by God, they just...

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So we got mad, all three of us, because he just...

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That was there when Graham was runnin' that when I was a farmer at my dad's place. And that was during the year...

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Wait a minute now. I was only farming... Oh yeah, let's see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Wait a minute.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I thought that Herbert...

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: There was a talk of... Oh, it was the 1918 war, I guess, when they was talkin' of that bread rationing.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You remember you had to eat dark bread?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It was talkin' of it anyway. And I go down to this Graham, this fellow that had the Gress Mill.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I didn't want to eat that black stuff that they was talkin' about. And I had my own wheat.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I said, how about gettin' you to grind up some flour?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He said, you can have all the flour you want, Mr. Ringside. He says, they can't take it away from you. He says, you can have it. So you got your own wheat. He says, you're welcome. So that's the way it went. I got a whole... I don't know. Enough to last the whole winter, see. Cause we didn't have no car. We used to want to run to town every time we run out of a sack of flour. So that's what we did.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And the wife, she liked...

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, this hard wheat Marcus wheat was good for bread, but it wasn't good for cakes. So she wanted the soft wheat flour for cakes. And I raised both kinds.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I had Marcus, was the hard wheat, and 40 fold they call it, or gold coin. That was a fall wheat, strictly fall wheat, and that was soft wheat.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And that made off a good straw for the cattle and horses too. It was kind of sweet joints, see. You cut it a little bit on the green side. With a binder you could cut stuff greener than you could with combine, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And so that way we'd get by a lot cheaper. You had to feed your... I had seven horses to feed.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That's what I farmed with. Two bottom gang.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I run that whole doggone place all by my loans. If I didn't hire... Well, I did... One year I did get a combine in there and I got disgusted with him.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He just poked around. He had an old hope that wasn't any good. He was breaking down all the time.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So the next year I got me an eight foot binder. I'd done my own binder, my own shocker. I'll tell you what I'd done. I cut 47 acres. It laid good. It was a half mile long.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: 47 acres and cut it and shocked it in two days and a half.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: The way I did, I had seven horses like I told you. I put four horses on, four o 'clock in the morning. I'd go out and cut till eight o 'clock. Then I'd come in and eat breakfast.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: My wife was cooking then. But this one guy, he was a young horse, and I called him Foxy and he was Foxy and I worked him both shifts. So I laid the other three off to rest till the next day and then I had three fresh ones and this Fox to go rest away till noon.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: In the afternoon I'd go out and shock up but I cut there in eight hours.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I could set a shock up at two and two, you know, is the way we shocked.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I dumped six bundles here and six there and I'd just step in between and I could just average the shock a minute.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I was shocked.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I was young then.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Man, around 18, I was 30. Just in prime of life, you know. And I was determined to make it go. Well now, Dad knew what I was or he wouldn't have read it to me. He had to back me in the first place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When was the first year that you farmed?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, 1918.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I moved in the fall of 17.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Started in. And the oldest daughter wasn't born until in 18.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: The very first year that I was farmed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You married at 15, right?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh no, hell no, 13.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you worked out before then, right?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, yeah. I worked out. You were telling me about that, so we already got that. Yeah, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But was the, when you were working out at these places, was the idea then, were you thinking of just making enough so you could start farming yourself?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I wasn't thinking, I was just doing the best I could, making as much as I could.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That's the way it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did I tell you about it?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I tried to get, I could have got a place rented from a neighbor there, a settler.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: One year, if Dad would have backed me for $1200 for the outfit, but he didn't, I don't know why he didn't, but he just, I think he had in mind to wait until he wanted it rented out and he wanted me on the place. I don't know that, he didn't say that, but I just figured he must have.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But he turned me down on that, and so I didn't get it. So then I just worked here and there wherever I could. Well I worked one fall there, we worked on the grade, when they built that new grade, Central Ridge grade down to Peck, that's about a seven mile grade.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you get the money to get your, to get the outfit that you used on your dad?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well I told you, I was farming, he backed me.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He just took a note.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your father did?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, my father took a note.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I, I, and I, You took his outfit?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well I had a team of my own that I had to do a little farming there for one year for Oscar Nelson. And so I had that one team of my own, but I wanted five more.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I told Dad I wanted seven horses on this twelve inch, fourteen inch plow, gang plow, I only stood with just six so I could, when the plowing was I could keep going, see, without wearing the dickens out of my horses. I took care of my horses, I had good fat horses, believe you me, they got feed whether I made any money or not. And they showed, they helped me out too.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: When I got out in the field, boy, I done a day's work.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I wasn't fooling around. Because of that, I gave them advantage. See, I wasn't, now, neighbor over there, Powell, oh I felt so sorry for them. He would starve them and he'd whip them and starve them and whip them, you know, trying to make the road. I couldn't take it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No, sir, I figured that's all the poor dumb roots get is what they eat.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And if they ain't worth that, we just better quit. That's the way I did it. I had a lot of good horses. I bought five from Dad and I trained them in pretty good. Well, there was one now mare that got her dad, she was a little stubborn, but I had to lick her a little bit.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But she was a dandy.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: She lived to be, oh, let's see, 20 -some years old.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And she would have still been 11 if it hadn't been she got pounders. See, after I went to raising hogs, I had been on the south side of the driveway, that big barn, you see there. And there's a little leak, I guess a knothole, spun a leak.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And she'd bite the rest of the horse away and he kept eating, see. And she went away and she got pounder and she was putting her gall and laying in the barn all bloated up. And I've discovered, you see, I had to shoot her. It's the only thing I could do.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So that's the way it went. And then away...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the deal that you and your father made when you started?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I think maybe you told me this. You told me when you quit, but I don't know so much when you started. Well, you see, when I started, gosh, I didn't have anything. I had to just charge stuff at stores. Well, I guess I had a little cash money to buy groceries to start with.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I owed him, let's see, I'll put in your $2 ,100. And then there was 19, when I had the big bean crop, see, that's what lifted me pretty good.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: The time that I had 44 acres of beans, I got $2 ,100 worth of beans.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But I couldn't give it all to Dad, but I paid him some, what I cut and what I owed the bank.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Now, when I got, let's see, what year was that?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It was 19, I guess, that I started raising, no, it was later than that.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: About 1923 is when I had to borrow some money to push my hogs through. Oh yeah. See, and so I told the old dogs, I had 105 pigs, 14 sows brought me 109. Four of them died, that's all. I raised 105, because I'd been to university years, I said, that's where that helped me out.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: How to diet my hogs and what kind of stock to judge to handle, don't you see?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I was smart enough to listen and learn, I was old enough to cut out foolish thoughts, so I had to concentrate on getting some place.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So, anyway, I stayed with it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I told this dog, I said, now I said, we're going to have $500 feeding these hogs through until I get my own crop, because I hadn't stored any ahead, I didn't. So he said, well, he said the bank had been broke and he was elected president, but he was just a farmer that was elected.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he said, I'll just have to see the directors, if they'll cater to it, all right.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So I said, okay.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So, I golly, he said, I'll come up tomorrow. I said, okay, don't. And see, he knew my dad and me, there was old timers there.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, his wife was the first person, you might say, a young person that I met in Pack. We stayed at Third Place, Warlick's. And she was just a girl, I guess, she was a little older than I was, of course, but she was old enough to be pretty talkative. And I was only a kid, eight years old, but I could remember it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So, yeah, she became his wife.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, anyway, so those come up the next morning, and when they come, you know, these pictures, they happen to be a sunshiney day. They were these red hogs, go to New York jerseys. And they got scared when they smelled this stranger coming, see. They run right up this ramp.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They just filled it, it was about a ramp about, well, it was big enough to hold them, put them near all of them. And they was all filled full, this must have been 20 foot long.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And they were in nice shape, they were getting put near to the weaning age, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They weighed around, oh, maybe 30 pounds apiece, something like that. Just nice roly -poly, you know, regular butterballs.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So he seen that and he says, well, he says, you sure didn't tell no fib. He said, I'll call the directors and see, he says, I'll call you back in the morning, see if you can get the money.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And sure enough, next morning, next morning I got the money, $500 to get some feed. Now, fed them through the fall. You were telling me this before, but I'm thinking, you never told me that you was living in Peck.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why were you living in Peck?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, hell, we just stayed there while Dad went moving his stuff up on the ridge. We didn't live there. No, we never lived there. We just stopped there till Dad could get his stuff moved up on the ridge. That's why I told you. He moved it on two wagon wheels. Going up there, and I was the boy that was with him. And he had a little old cabin, dirt floor, and he had sourdough there in the cabin and a piece of fat pork.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That's all we had for breakfast. He threw it up in the ceiling, he was going to show me how good he was, and he lost it and it went down in the dirt, but he whipped it up. It tasted good. We was good and hungry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever go to the town when you were growing up much? Did you go into Peck when you were growing up?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh, we were there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is there any use that you could have in town?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Dancers, sure, we went there. The dancers, us young fellers, sure we would. And shortly after Mother died, while the neighbors were doing Fourth of July, the hymns, it was a hymn with these boys that we were chums with, that I had a battle with one of them.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: We might say palled together, and she knew it and she just felt sorry for us because we was motherless. And she invited us to go with them down to Peck at the Fourth of July celebration. We'd probably have a dime or something like that and buy a little lemonade or a firecracker or two. You know, you'd get a firecracker package them days for a nickel, some of them. And some of them was a dime. And we didn't get many nickels, I tell you, from Dad them days.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He only made, well the first year that he went out to harvest, he only made $35.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I tell you, that's why I say he had a hard role. If he didn't have a lot of determination to make a go of it, he would have never made it. That's why I look back on the way he struggled. And the way he, now he lived there about only 30 years and look how he improved that place.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I was in skaters, got him interested and fenced in a place hog tight, mind you. And he never raised a hog until after I had raised him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, Helmer, do you think he, would you say looking back on it that he expected a lot of his kids, that he expected the kids to do what?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, I just was determined to do something because he, the way he kept, uh, Decembering us kids, I think he had in mind that was it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That he wanted to see us get someplace, but he just wouldn't let no nonsense get in the way. He was, whenever we kids tried to be foolish, he'd, look out.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, you said, when you talked about it, you said you kids were little devils. Well, I don't know, them days I guess, there was more vitamins in the vegetables and all. And that one thing, well listen, dad, dad did, he had, he raised pork.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You couldn't have beef them days, it was mostly pork.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he cured the pork by putting it in similar sacks, sowed it up in similar sacks, smoked it and put it in similar sacks and put it in an oat bin.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of sack is this then?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Seemless sacks, seamless sacks. Oh yeah. They're kind of a heavy sack that would hold Timothy's seed, what they used them for, mostly.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, uh, he would sow it up in that and put it in the oat bin to keep the meat from, bugs. And the oat bin kind of kept it cool too, seeing hot weather in the barn, it was in the bins, grain bins. And, uh, so, uh, that way he, uh, he always had plenty to eat. He had meat, pork, that is, bacon and ham and we'd boil the hams a lot of times, we'd make stew you might call it, or soup. And he had learned, I guess, from my mother, to make dumplings, we'd make dumplings and potatoes and carrots and stuff, vegetables. We, we, we raised a lot of good garden. Them days that soil was very good for garden. And he planted orchard, you can see that on the picture.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, so you, you kids were, you think you kids were so sassy and mischievous because you made them so good?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, you know, we just, uh, I don't know, it's just kind of natural, I guess, for kids that certain age that they want to play.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I guess I told you about that one time that, uh, he'd always take a nap at noon hours, see, when he's harvesting, cutting with a binder.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And, uh, that's before we got so we could get out in the shock, I guess.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But we was picking currants.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Now I, he taught me how to make jelly out of currants.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I was supposed to pick them, see, and then first, then cook them on the stove.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And so, uh, he, uh, take his nap. Well, I got the idea. I was, I guess I was a little bit too meds native.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I told the boys, I said, let's go down the saddle, he's raising horses. That's another way that he got made a go of it, is raising horses. And them days of horse prices was good. And, uh, he raised a lot of them. He had, oh, six or eight colts sometimes a year. So, anyway, uh, I said, let's go down and put a saddle on him. He had a saddle. I said, let's go down and put a saddle on one of them colts, see, buck.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Keeps going down. That was where he got this barn. That was just a shed barn that he had. And haystack was stacked, was all out in the yard, and they were fenced with wire, barbed wire, see. So, we got this colt set up, and, you know, they did get the same shop height as we could, and he was too small. And the colt, we had it on the colt, and he got excited and started running to get his foot in the stirrup and turned the saddle underneath him.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And about that time, here dad come running down through the orchard hollering, you know. About that time, I was making a streak for the curts.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he told me, he said, no, I hadn't, wait now. So, they got the, they did get, I guess the boys got the colt and the barn all right.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Because they got, everything went fine, never hurt the colts. You see, he could have got in the wire, but he didn't. But anyway, dad was pretty peeved.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he come up there to me and he said, now, Helmer, you go out down there and get a great big long switch. Good limber one, he'd tell you.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he said, I'll see to you.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Before I come back in with God, I knew I had to do, I was guilty.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I had to tell him, well, he says, he seen that, and that kind of worked on him.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, he says, Helmer, if you'll pick these curts and never do that again, he says, I'll let you go this time.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yes, yes, yes, I said, I didn't come.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So, I said, that's where dad put the pep into me.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Right, that one lesson.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Now, wouldn't you, wouldn't you think it?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It seems reasonable.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He just, he just cooked my ..., just right there, see, for doing that devilry.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, brother, he didn't seem to do that. He let you off, which was kind of merciful, really. It was kind of merciful, really, he let you off. I was, but he did. And I tell you, he gave by it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He made a man out of me. And he finally decided, when, you see, when it come to rentin' then, he knew what I was. He tested me out every way, shape, and form. He knew for me, you see. Well, I'd had fights after I grew up. I had fights with all kinds of kids there. But he didn't care then after I, well, there was one of them he did.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I was only 14 then. And we got to Sunday school, Steiner and I, that's my oldest brother.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And this gal here, he was a cousin to the Hardman boys. I tell you about the Hardman boys.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he got me down on the floor right in the schoolhouse. That was his schoolhouse where we went to Sunday school, see. And he bled my nose, boy, and I had my white shirt on, you know, and I was a cryin'. I could help myself.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He was a grown man just about. But he was crippled, he had one leg game.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: We, Steiner and I, Dad hadn't got there yet. And Steiner and I started down the hill toward where Dad was comin'.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And Dad said, what's the matter?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, Steiner and I told him that gal of your boy jumped onto me and bled my nose.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And boy, that made him more than mad. He went up on the porch and said, you boys ain't supposed to come here to fight. He said, you're supposed to come here and be peaceable and go into Sunday school or church.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He said, if you want to fight, come on, the whole bunch of you. He didn't mind the whole bunch of you. There was some pretty big Hardman boys too. He was getting up there quite as big as Dad. Well, this gal there was. And I had made up my mind if I could ever catch that cousin. I never could fix him when I growed up, but I never could find him.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But I did meet these two brothers, these two brothers, see, Hardman boys. I fought both of them. And I let them go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was to get back?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No, no, no, no, listen.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: The youngest one, we all got into it with him. He started it. I wasn't starting it. He started it. I and this August Picard, as a French boy, we were coasting over on his place. And this Dennis Wittock that I'd had a little trouble with, and this Owen Hardman was coming along, what they called Jean, not Jean, Emmett Goughton's place.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It was about three quarters of a mile and you could just barely hear them hollering.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They hollered over and called me sons of bitches. And I wanted this French kid, I said, let's go over there and clean up on them bastards. And he was a coward. He wouldn't do it. And I said, I'll sure fix it. I'll get him at school sometime. After school, I meant. And I said, sure enough, one day he'd come.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, the way that started, though, mainly was, Dad had heard that him and Dennis Wittock, he was going to kill our dog, Carlos.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he was riding a horseback right past our place and Dad was down there by the crunch bushes. Doing something, I guess maybe picking some.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he overheard them. And that they was going to shoot our dog when they come back.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: They'd go down the ridge a ways, see, and then come back and shoot them as they come by.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who's going to do this now?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: These two, Dennis and Owen Hardman.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And so Dad, they'd loaded. Steiner's got the shotgun there. He had the repeater, 12 gauge.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he just shot in the air, of course. He didn't shoot the kid, but he shot in the air and scolded them. And told them, by gosh, they'd better get on home and not try to kill your dog. And after he'd done that, he'd run in the house. He had a phone them days. And he'd run in the house and he called up Wittock and Hardman. Told them where their boys was and he says, Wittock says, Hidden was up with Owen Hardman. Hardman says, Hidden was with the Wittock kid. Well, he says, I'm going to tell you guys to take care of your boys or he says, I will. He says, he's trying to kill my dog. Well, then the next day when I come to school, this little Owen Hardman, see, he got smart.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He just stood there with a, they had a pin on his coat them days. He was kind of dressed up, you know, just having a look a little at it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He had a kind of resentful look at me anyway in the first place, see. And I guess that's why I was egotistic enough to kind of look a little, maybe staring at this pin. So he had his hand behind him, and he's like, Green, I said, that's a pretty pin, I think that's why I told him.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He says, you see anything green in there, you heard it, you knew it heard us. And I said, no, I don't know.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he says, well, I said, what are you doing with that hand back there?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He had it drawed back like he was going to do something.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Said, there's going to be one left in the, that was the one before we changed our name, Swenson.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: One left in the Swenson family's drug house. He said, let's go out on the porch out here if you want something like that.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I invited him right away. Well, because they're teaching doctors right there. I said, that was too close.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So I told my friends, I said, Arthur Stamper and Carl Hardin, I said, I'm going to have to get him off the ground I guess sometime. I said, you better look out. Said, he's going to have a knife for you, drawed a knife.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I said, that won't stop me. I said, I ain't going to get bluffed out like that. That son of a bitch is going to take that back. I said, one or the other. So I'm going to find out who called me, whether it's him or Dennis. If it's Dennis, I'll take it out of him. If it's Owen, I'll take it out of him.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And so one day, and he wore a great big white cowboy hat. One day, it happened.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Because the boy said, I'm going to follow him today.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he had a little shed for his pony.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He rode to school. He lived up the ridge quite a ways, three or four miles.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So I got up there and I said, Owen, which one of you boys called me and Pecot Kid?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That son of a bitch name the other day, the other Sunday, I said, well, we were coasting over here. Me, by God, he'd drawn up like this. I was standing just like this with my hand in my pocket.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said, Owen, you're going to take that back?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He said, no, I got that one. I got him right square in the eye. See, he blacked his eye right away. And then he, he, see, I guess he decided I better, well, he brought his knife.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: That's what he did. He jerked back and got his knife out. And of course, I wasn't going to fight him with his knife out.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I run around behind his little shed and in just luck, I happened to find a stick about that big around and foot long.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He said, now drop your knife, I said, unless fight is out there. Well, then the school mom would find out we were fighting up there, you know.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So we was called down to the school and then she thought, she didn't know what to do. She had to call the directors.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So we had, I had, well, this Doug Hunter was a, he hated, he knew what the Harbin boys was, see. But Whittett, of course, he was on the side of the Harbin. But Stanley, he was neutral. He was three directors.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So anyway, they asked Owen, you know, how did the fight start?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, he said, he asked me if I called him a name and he said, I told him I did and then he up and hit me.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, that ought to be enough that I was in the right. But no, Whittett said, he thought that I ought to be canned, without him being canned, see. Well, Doug Hunter didn't think so. And Stanley, he says, I'll tell you, the way I look at it is, they both ought to be, he said, they're both in trouble. They've got to both be off. Well, that's the way they decided it then, finally, that we both had to be off a week. We was canned a week from school. I come back, but he didn't. He stayed off, I think, two or three weeks.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And so that saddled that, of course.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But he finally, now just to show you how hunters, they sure hated him, but I'll be doggoned, gone if that Owen didn't marry the oldest daughter of hunters. And they resented, they just didn't like it. God, the time goes by.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You'll have to come back again if we go to get it all. I'll come back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, in a way then, huh? This going to school over there, did you pretty much like that, going to school, or was it kind of a waste of your time?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, here's the deal.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: When we were that young and had so much chore work to do at home, how could you get interested when you'd get started way late and be behind? You wouldn't know what the heck to think, don't you see?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: We just kids.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: You know how you'd be when you're in a place like that. You'd better shut that off now. No, go ahead. Well, that's the way, in a way.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And we just done the best we could.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But when you say, yeah, do all the chore and everything, that means you couldn't go to school as much as, you had to start late in the fall because you was working in the... And it was only three months a year, I told you, several times, that that's all the schooling that they had those days. Just those eight, three months for several years. I guess the time when they got the schoolhouse done, yeah, they had more then, but I didn't get to go quite so much.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: But we got as far as to the, like I said, the seventh, I think I was in the seventh, but I didn't finish it because I didn't Spokane College. That's the way that went.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, you know, one thing I was wondering about... One thing I was wondering about was the difference between being the neighboring on Central Ridge and over at Park. You went over to Park later and I was wondering whether, it sounds like in some ways maybe the people weren't as friendly on Central Ridge as they were. Oh, we had a lot of good friends on the Ridge. We boys, we were welcome as could be because we lost our mother. When my aunt was doing the cooking, that was dad's youngest sister.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Go to Dancis and have a big time. Neighborhood would go up to Mola, the neighbors up there, and they'd come down to our either school house or neighbors' places to dance.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And all the neighbor women we could dance with, all of them, they just enjoyed dancing with us because we were motherless. And well, Mrs.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Malcolm, her husband didn't dance.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So I got to dance with her. Now it's only their children that's left over on Central Ridge. I go over there, but believe it or not, but our cemetery where my folks are buried is right on, happened to be on this one place that they bought.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: These children's dad bought this place where this acre lot where the Central Ridge Cemetery is for the pioneers. And we go over there, I got three granddaughters, this daughter's daughters, takes me over there every year to clean that one thing they don't have over there. Now it's a rich country, but you see it's too, now it's gobbled up with big bankers and rich farmers who's got great big places and they don't give a hoot for it. The old pioneers are gone. Now you take this little cemetery and park, the county takes care of it, not over there. So we go over there every year and my granddaughters, they promised that when I'm gone they was going to keep it up, keep taking care of it. Our dad was smart enough to get a big lot, a regular family lot, right up again the gate, mind you. It's just south of the gate and he planted two red fir trees, and they're great big trees now. See that was after mother passed away about 19, 1 or 2, somewhere along there, so they'd be 75 years old now, or 6 or 7, 8, no it'd be 75, 74 or 5 I mean, if it was 19, 2. Yeah they're big tall trees right at the corner, southeast corner of the burial lot. And there he's got these twins buried, you see she had twins. And that was after Steiner, in between Simon and Steiner, they would have been, what's the other, 86, they would have been 83 now. And then the youngest brother, he was only 36, not quite 36 when he died, they're buried there, them twins, the youngest brother and mother and dad.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I had, when dad, after dad died, he had put one tombstone for mothers. And that was leaning, it just happened to be a slab, about so thick, and about so wide and about 3 or 4 feet tall.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And that was starting to tip.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So I goes over to these neighbors, Malcolm's, they lived right there, well we went there for dinner, they invited us over. And I said Roy, I said, have you got a big angle of harness, and I'd like to get to put under my mother's tombstone, it's starting to tip.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I said if I don't do something it's going to be tipping right over on lay on the ground. See them days they used them tall ones, big ones, now they got them already flat on the ground. So yeah, he said I might find something, he looked around, he found me a good big angle.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So that was the day that I had bought tombstones from this Moscow guy, and he went with me, took me over there. I'd bought tombstones from my youngest brother and my dad. Dad had put for the twins, and mother that's right, I forgot about that.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: The twins had tombstones, but it was dad and my youngest brother that I bought tombstones, and was getting put up, see, the time that I fixed that mother's, straightened that up. So that's the way that went. And that still stayed, my gosh, to this day, that's tombstone to stand level, just like I fixed it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, Helmer, you were talking about when you were farming over there, did you, when you farmed, did you believe at all in the planting by the signs and all that business?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I didn't enter my head then. I was too doggone busy farming, and I had my mind full, planted full with the work I was doing, you see. I had to concentrate on that. Well, I tell you, I was busy. Like I told you, I'm not doing all that.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I done pretty good. A lot of them told me, they said, well that fall that I quit, I was just a walking skeleton.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I didn't weigh, I guess about 130, 135 pounds.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And that's why I quit. The reason I quit was I had, I could see it was the hog money.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Hogs come up in good price. I sold half of them for 8 1 3 2, and the other half for 10 1 3 2.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And the wheat come up to a dollar and a half.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I told my dad, I said, I only owned dad about $1 ,160 left, is all I owed him.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I said, dad, I'd like to just quit and cancel, give you all the outfit I got here now, all but the one team, give you back.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I had raised two colts, young colts, and extra machinery.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I said, there's a $100 .00 a hundred dollar gas motor and a binder and a drill that you didn't have.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I said, oh shit, it's bigger. That 8 foot binder, you had a 6 foot. I said, here's an 8 foot, and here's a 10 foot drill you didn't have. And I said, here's a good motor and a chop mill. There's all that. And some brood shops. I said, I'll give you all that. Oh no, first he wouldn't do it, you know. He's going to try to keep me there. He liked the way I was doing it. I'd given him, oh, he got, I think it was a couple years there, he got $2 ,000 cash rent from me. And, so no, he says, oh you're doing all right. And the bank did too. Bank didn't even want it. Said I could better keep going. I was just getting, you're just getting good start. Yes, but I said, unless something could happen, I wouldn't.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, there's one thing, I had a neighbor there. Yeah, you told me about it. And he told me about quitting too. He let you, you made him let you, you went. Yeah, I quit because I told him I just determined, I just got that in my head. Well, here's some, there's a lot of things happened. See, my wife got sick that spring. I just had a tuffet.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: She brought birth to a dead baby boy, weighed 10 pounds. She went overtime, was it 10 months until her 9. She damn near died too. And that boy, my oldest boy was only 8, and the oldest daughter only 6. And the other one 4, and I was, man, I tell you, she passed out and I thought I was left with them 3. And I said, I don't want no more of this here, or trouble. And I said, if I sell out everything, I can clean it up and I don't want to get out. Go to work someplace else.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: This fireman don't suit me one bit. And the next years it turned rainy weather. Twenty, not twenty -five, but twenty -six, twenty -seven, if I had stayed there, I would have went broke there instead of over the park. Well, Helmer, I tell you, I'd like it if you would tell me some about when you finally decided to leave this country and go over to the coast and how that was. Yeah, well, now that's when I started. After Dad died, now I'm going to get into where I thought we were going to start in the first place. Yeah, well, after Dad died, that was in 19, he died in 35, May 28th.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, we didn't get the, he hadn't sold the place. He hadn't sold it, he'd give it away for $10 ,000. That's what he did, that whole big farm. That fella was smart, but Dad was always scared he was going to go broke, see. He seen the farmers in Genesee country in the early days go broke by buying extra land and mortgage in their homestead and they'd lose the whole works. So he was worried.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And he wasn't, he wasn't a man that was getting filled by it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He didn't get the operation then he didn't. That's what they did operate when he died. It was the tumors that grow that big and he's shut his water off. Shut his water off.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I was right there when it happened.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I told my uncle Adolph and Charlotte, his wife, and my stepmother, see Dad married again after several years, 1910 I guess it was, 8 -9 years after Mother died. And she was a good worker, good cook, good housekeeper. But she had a lot of spunk and they didn't get along too well, but that was alright. It was his fault, I admit that. He was careless. She would have meals right on the dump and she'd call him and he'd be waiting, waiting, waiting, making her irritable. That ain't no good. That was his fault. Yeah, he wanted to finish. After he got a cook, I didn't even appreciate that part of it. That was the only place that I could say that I didn't like it. That's what I was looking for, was when he'd done that. She had her faults too, alright, but I got along with her. Just to show you, she was catering to me for to be the administrator instead of Uncle Ad. So that's how they come to get by.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Anyway,

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: he died in 1935. In 1938, we had to sell with a reduction, a $300 reduction to get him to pay cash.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: The farmer that bought, he was a little farmer on Raymond Park, on Central Ridge.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And we took the reduction. Well then, we, oh I got out of it. For all the struggle I had was this $1200, my share of this day, my, oh I didn't tell you, they adopted a little baby girl.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: When they lived in Seattle, see, and she'd growed up, and Dad and Stepmother were schooling her over there to park. And then she, she, when she became heir. So this lawyer we hired was a, no for good, he was a, Shimpke was his name. And he was, I didn't know he was a cook until the next year we found it out afterwards.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: He says, he allotted her $2000 and we boys didn't get nowhere near to that, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well we got $1200 and my brother Steiner, I told him, I said, the way I figured, whoever got the park please. Now the Stepmother was pretty generous there. Dad told us that she was pretty reasonable. Said, see, you boys better not try to have any fights because if you go to fighting with law you'll lose it all. That's why he had cautioned us that beforehand, see. So we were smart enough to know that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what with his will did he make you all eat?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No he didn't make no will.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No will?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No will. So,

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: but the old lady, I talked with her, she said, well I'll tell you. She said, if I can get the Kirkland property, see Dad had bought some property in Kirkland, Washington.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It's out of, I guess, northeast, the main city is Seattle. And, yeah we all agreed. And she said, whoever gets, wants the park place, she's willing to, she could have got, she could have held more, you know, she'd have wanted to. No, she said, I'll let you boys have that. She said, we can't make no living on it, we didn't. And she says, times was down man, at that time, you see. That's why she was that generous, I guess.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: So she moved out there, and so I made the suggestion to the boys, we was all together there, to agree, by the lawyer, you know, that if, I said like this, I said, I'll take that as my share or someone else, you boys, any one of you, want that park place for your share.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I said, if anybody goes in that, I figured they couldn't make it. They'd go broke. And I wasn't willing to about take it on my hand for that, but Steiner, that lived up in Canada at the time, he just grabbed, even told his wife a fib about what a good place it was, you know, just to get her consent.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, he'd been farming up in Canada? No, yeah, he lived in Canada for, well wait now, I gotta figure, is it in 1916?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And this was 1938.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where at was he farming? Was he in San Francisco or Alberta?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well, he was up at around Wainwright, Alberta, or something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So was he homesteading up there?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: No, yeah, he'd loaned Uncle Ed some money up there. Steiner was working in the mines up there, making money, good money, at that time. And so Ed got in debt to Steiner, so Ed turned some land over to him, that's how he just started farming.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But he really wanted to come back to the park?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Oh yeah, yeah, he sold out up there then, and after he found out, the boys all, we all agreed, we just signed that place over to him. And they come down, they had quite a struggle, they couldn't get on relief at that time, but her mother, his wife's mother, she helped them out that winter. Well, I didn't tell you all that. The old lady was good enough, she had five or six milk cows that she turned over to them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your stepmother?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Yeah, her stepmother did, so they could be selling little cream to get a little grocery money. So she done, by God darn, pretty good. And there was a cow used to team to do little ground scratching, you might call it, about all it amounted to. But they got by for a few years, and that's how come he and I, in 1936, we was out cutting logs together. Uh huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what did you do? What did you come out with then?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well then, yeah, that's what I'm saying. Then I got this cash money, see, $1200, and I was working on relief down here in Moscow.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I was only getting $40 a month to feed my family.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Pounding rocks out here, crushing rocks out here. Them days, they didn't have a street down here on the lowest street.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It was muddy, it rained 1934, it rained all winter.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It took four horses to pull a wagon that was sunken down halfway on the spokes to go through and dump these rocks in this mud, see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I was out there pounding them rocks to get them through the crusher. $40 a month. And so that went on for a few years, you see.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And when I got this money then in 1938, well then I had a penicillitis operation too.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I had to go in depth for that. We were renting then, that was before, just before I got the place. That was last year before I got this little place in Moscow. Before we got our air money, you might say. 19, that was about 19, yeah, 38, when I was operating on.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And I was laying there renting down on Kenneth Avenue, paying rent. And off so bad I'd been setting pins for to live, make a little living extra money, besides relief money, for a fellow running a pool, I mean a bowling alley down here in Moscow.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: 35 cents a line's all that guy, you see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: 35 cents a line?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: A line, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's a line? Oh, then they're, you know they have, what is it now? An alley?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: A line on the scoreboard. It's a line to go, they count 10. Right, right. Yeah. And it's one of them lines, nearest I can remember, it's the way he paid it.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: And whatever that, I don't know, I didn't get very damn much.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: Well then one winter, wait now, I'm getting too far. So then, let's see now, yeah, I got operated on in 38. And finally long, got to working long, and I told the boys, I kept talking, you know, oh boy, I said I'd like to get a place, but I said I didn't have much money. I said I finally got $1200 cash from my estate money.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: One guy spoke up and he says, I've got an aunt here in town, he says, got a two acre tract out here in Moscow, he said, East Ponce Coast. Over there to the other side of D Street, on E Street. He says, she'll probably sell it to you. I said, well, I want to see her. I said, Sharp was her name.

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: I went over there, yeah, cash, you know, that talked. And I said, boy, I'll just flop that old check over for that place. It was small five room house, very small, it was cold, only one boat shack, but there's a big barn on it. And dad had given me a cow, that's right, for doing some work for him. So we had our own cow and milk. And I put that into Boysenberry's and the first four years I sold $400 a year worth of Boysenberry's off of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, what was the, where was it at now, it was on E Street?

speaker: Helmer Ringsage
words: It's East E Street, yeah, it's still there, but oh, it's gone, it's on big, big business there you might say, there's a big housing department. But I kept it, I kept it for four years. I got that in 38 and in 42 we decided to move to that big ward business in Portland. I had this brother of mine down there, Simon, he had his own home bought, that was the one that had, died with cancer when he was 64. He was living in Portland. He was living in the city.
